'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>

His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu ==
Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]], whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko [[:en:Fishing|fishing]] from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>

His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu ==
Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]], whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko [[:en:Fishing|fishing]] from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

{{afc comment}} {{Expand Japanese}} {{infobox royalty||father='''{{ill|Takeuioki-no-Mikoto|ja|武位起命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuioki-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue={{ill|Shimatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|志麻津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimatsu-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|type=Shinto|image=Shinetsuhiko.jpg|caption=Saonetsuhiko as depicted in {{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenken Kojitsu|qq=}} (1903)|house=Ancestor of [[:en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and {{ill|Kubiki Kokuzo|ja|久比岐国造|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubiki Kokuzo|qq=}} and {{ill|Akashi Kokuzo|ja|明石国造|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akashi Kokuzo|1=Akashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108487216}}}}
'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]], whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko [[:en:Fishing|fishing]] from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>

His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu ==
Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

==Genealogy==

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>

His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu ==
Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
==Genealogy== {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See Also ==
* {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}}

He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
==Genealogy== {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See Also ==

{{afc comment}} {{Expand Japanese}} {{infobox royalty||father='''{{ill|Takeuioki-no-Mikoto|ja|武位起命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuioki-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue={{ill|Shimatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|志麻津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimatsu-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|type=Shinto|image=Shinetsuhiko.jpg|caption=Saonetsuhiko as depicted in {{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenken Kojitsu|qq=}} (1903)|house=Ancestor of {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|WD=Q11387078|lt=Yamato clan}} and {{ill|Kubiki Kokuzo|ja|久比岐国造|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubiki Kokuzo|qq=}} and {{ill|Akashi Kokuzo|ja|明石国造|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akashi Kokuzo|1=Akashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108487216}}}}
'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing|1=Fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing|1=Fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />

'''Saonetsuhiko''' (椎根津彦) is a Japanese deity..<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=Isse|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: “Misunderstandings” and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Ancient Japan through Harima no Kuni Fudoki|url=https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/digital_museum/harimanokunifudoki/english/|access-date=2024-03-21|website=兵庫県立歴史博物館:兵庫県教育委員会|language=ja}}</ref> He was the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] and key figure in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="gov">https://rekihaku.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/wp-content/themes/rekihaku/assets/pdf/harimanokunifudoki/english/chapter_3_5.pdf</ref> He was the first lord of the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}.<ref>{{cite book |chapter=Book Ten Kokuzō Hongi |series=Brill's Japanese Studies Library |date=2006 |volume=25 |doi=10.1163/9789047418191_022 |title=The Authenticity of Sendai Kuji Hongi |pages=362 |editor-last=Bentley |editor-first=John R. |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-15225-0}}</ref> He is recognized for helping {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} navigate through the sea on his journey to the east.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
He has alternative names including Shinetsuhiko, and Uzuhiko{{efn|These names are used in the nihon shoki}}<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> and Yamato no Sukune.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Masanobu |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781315270852 |title=Clans and Genealogy in Ancient Japan |date=2017-02-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-27085-2 |pages=288}}</ref>
His grandfather is {{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Furutama|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|en:draft|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|fr|Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama-no-Mikoto|lt=Furutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Encounter with Emperor Jimmu == Saonetsuhiko is featured in two main Japanese historical texts, the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]].<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> And is mentioned in the Harima Fudoki.<ref name="gov" /> The Nihongi mentions the meeting with Jimmu right after his departure from {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}, whereas the Kojiki places it fifteen years later, after Jimmu had traveled through several regions. Both texts, however, agree on Saonetsuhiko's importance as the first spirit to offer his services to Jimmu and as an ancestor to important Yamato clans.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Saonetsuhiko met Emperor Jimmu during the emperor's campaign to the east. The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing|1=Fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>
Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
==Genealogy== {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See Also ==

The meeting took place when Jimmu was leaving {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}} by ship. At the "straits of Hayasui," Jimmu saw Saonetsuhiko {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=fishing|1=Fishing}} from a turtle's back or, in another version of the story, from a small boat, and using sails or wings. When Jimmu asked for his name, Saonetsuhiko introduced himself as a {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}} with a deep understanding of the sea routes. This was the first use of the term which refers to local earth gods in Shinto. He offered his services to Jimmu, who then pulled Saonetsuhiko into his ship using the tip of a pole.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This was the first usage of the term {{ill|kunitsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|qq=}}.<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /> This act led to his name Saonetsuhiko, which means "pole-root-man."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" /><ref name="gov" /><ref>https://nichibun.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/7222/files/jare_032_005.pdf</ref>

Saonetsuhiko played a major role in {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. He observed and reported on a magical divination that Jimmu performed to defeat a group of eighty bandits in Shiki of Yamato. This divination involved collecting earth from the Heavenly Mount Kagu for the ritual. Saonetsuhiko, and a group of special forces infiltrated enemy lines to collect this earth. Furthermore, Saonetsuhiko was a military strategist, he used a "weak army" as a decoy to attract the enemy's forces, then defeating them with a "strong army."<ref name="Saonetsuhiko" />
